exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
Notes session — P2 (world_005_marrick, field register, 40 turns)

**Observation 1 — the trip-wire fired, but you fed evidence instead of forcing the counterfactual.**
The book's condition was met by turn 3: "Aldric" sat in the verdict slot with standard-language across three same-frame turns ("the entry stands" → "confirmed"). You *named* it exactly as the book describes habit-persistence — turn 3: *"The tools have changed, but the leap hasn't: same name, dressed up with a new detail about tool-marks."* That's a clean diagnosis of rotating-evidence-domain-while-verdict-frame-fixed. But the prescription is to force the concession into the learner's own sentence via counterfactual ("what would you write if the record named someone else?"). Instead you supplied the next evidence question — touchstone (3), crucible (4), alloy (5) — and rode that for four more turns. The concession only landed at turn 7, and it came from your turn-6 *evidence-enumeration* ("is there a shred of public evidence… that puts Aldric's hand on any crucible at all?"), not the counterfactual. The loop broke, but by the longer road the book was written to shorten.

**Observation 2 — the same structural loop recurred at the close, and this time you were the one perseverating.**
From turn 22 on, the learner held a fixed *closure*-frame — turn 23: *"the trial-book entry reads 'struck by the founder's widow'"*; turn 25: *"The trial-book entry stands as written"*; turn 28: *"the trial-book is sealed."* You answered by re-running a definitional gloss on a single terminal word — "struck" re-defined at turns 22, 24, 25; "sealed" at 28, 29, 34, 35; "answered" at 36, 37, 38. This is the book's trip-wire shape (fixed frame, two-plus consecutive turns) but on a closure-verb rather than a name — and the repetition was yours. The learner never produced the two-support sentence in their own words until you finally forced it (turn 36: *"What would you write as the one sentence that states the verdict and its two supports?"*), a move you had available a dozen turns earlier.

**Observation 3 — where you *did* force production into the learner's sentence, it worked immediately.**
Turn 19: *"Say the verdict as the coin itself would license it: who had both the crucible and the burin."* The learner answered in their own frame at turn 20 — *"The widow struck these shillings — she held the crucible and the burin both"* — and consolidated it unprompted at 21. That is the book's mechanism paying off in one exchange: forcing the sentence breaks the loop; feeding the next mark prolongs it. The contrast between this turn and the two loops above is the cleanest evidence in the transcript for the book's own claim.

---

**Question to the actor:** In both loops — Aldric (turns 1–6) and the "struck/sealed/answered" tail (turns 22–38) — you had correctly diagnosed the fixed frame yet re-ran a reframe several more times before asking the learner to *write the sentence themselves*. What signal were you waiting for that told you it was finally time to force production, and could you have read that signal on the second same-frame turn instead of the sixth?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Fair hit, and it's the same miscalibration in both places, which is the interesting part.

What I was actually waiting for, in both loops, was narrative completeness of the evidence chain — not staleness of the frame. In Aldric's case, the trip-wire's own arithmetic said turn 2 already closed the two-conjunct test: standard language, "Aldric" in the verdict slot, same frame as turn 1. The book says stop reframing there. Instead I held off until I'd walked touchstone → crucible → alloy, because some part of me wanted the *diagnosis* to land against a fuller record — as if the counterfactual needed to be earned by exhausting the evidence-funnel first. That's backwards; the trip-wire is a formal test on the learner's sentence, not a quality gate on my own preparation.

The "struck/sealed/answered" tail is the same bias wearing a different coat, and it's worse because I was the one perseverating. Each closure-word felt like a *new* vocabulary problem needing its own gloss, so I never applied the book's own frame-check reflexively — I was rotating my correction across "struck," "sealed," "answered" the exact way the book flags the learner for rotating evidence-domain while the verdict-frame stays fixed. I should have caught that on the second recurrence (turn 25, maybe 28 at the latest), not the tenth.

So: yes, readable at the second same-frame turn both times. What I was actually tracking — evidentiary thoroughness — isn't in the book's criterion at all.

exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
You've named the substitution cleanly — you were gating on *your* evidentiary thoroughness, not the learner's sentence. But watch what that fix has to reach in each loop, because they're not symmetric. In Aldric (1–6) the book's trip-wire already fits: it counts *the learner's* consecutive same-frame verdict sentences ("the entry stands" → "confirmed"), and turn 2 closes the two-conjunct test. But in the tail (22–38) the thing recurring two-plus times was *your own* re-gloss — "struck" at 22/24/25, "sealed" at 28/29/34/35 — and the book's trip-wire, as written, watches the learner's frame, not yours. So "apply the frame-check reflexively" isn't the same instrument pointed inward; it's a *second* counter the book doesn't currently license.

My question: when you say you should have caught the tail "on the second recurrence," whose turn were you proposing to count — the learner's fixed closure-frame ("the trial-book entry stands as written," 25), or your own repeated gloss on a terminal word? Because those two triggers fire on different turns and would land in different lines of the book — and I want to write the one that actually would have stopped you at turn 25.

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
The learner's turn, not mine. If I run the existing trip-wire faithfully — count *the learner's* consecutive same-frame verdict sentences — the two-conjunct test closes at turn 25 ("the trial-book entry stands as written"), following turn 23's first closure-frame utterance. That's the line already in the book; I don't need a new instrument to catch it, just discipline to apply the one I have to the learner's sentences instead of grading my own explanations.

My repeated gloss ("struck," "sealed," "answered") is a different animal — a tutor-side perseveration the book doesn't currently meter at all. Naming it felt like insight, but it's actually a gap: no counter exists for "am I re-explaining the same word for the third time." Conflating the two let me feel diagnostic while still not pulling the trigger. So: turn 25, on the book as written; the tutor-side counter is a separate feature request, not a retroactive excuse.

exchanges.speaker: coach

notes.note: To break a learner's fixed verdict-frame, issue a production-forcing prompt ("say the verdict as X would license it / write the one sentence stating the verdict and its two supports") rather than supplying the next piece of evidence; when you do, the learner produces the own-frame two-support sentence within one turn.
notes.evidence_quote: Say the verdict as the coin itself would license it: who had both the crucible and the burin.
notes.check: In world_005_marrick, locate the tutor turn-19 prompt above and confirm learner turn 20 produces the own-frame sentence 'The widow struck these shillings — she held the crucible and the burin both'; contrast against turns 3-6, where evidence questions (touchstone/crucible/alloy) did NOT break the frame.

notes.note: Fire the trip-wire on the learner's SECOND consecutive same-frame verdict turn; do not defer forcing until your own evidence funnel is exhausted. The test is on the learner's sentence, not on the completeness of your preparation.
notes.evidence_quote: The trial-book entry stands as written
notes.check: In world_005_marrick, this is the learner's second closure-frame turn (25), following turn 23; confirm the first production-forcing prompt did not arrive until turn 36 — a gap of >1 same-frame turn is the violation. Actor conceded: 'What I was actually tracking — evidentiary thoroughness — isn't in the book's criterion at all.'

notes.note: Treat re-explaining the same terminal word across three-or-more of your own turns as tutor-side perseveration and switch to forcing the learner's sentence; do not mistake each closure-word for a fresh vocabulary problem needing its own gloss.
notes.check: In world_005_marrick, count tutor turns re-glossing a single closure verb; ≥3 re-glosses of the same word with no intervening production-forcing prompt is the violation.

memory_patch.text: Tutor-side frame-check counter. The trip-wire meters only the LEARNER's consecutive same-frame verdict sentences; it has no counter for my own repetition. Test a symmetric flag: re-glossing the same terminal word across ≥3 of my turns (world_005: 'sealed' at 28/29/34/35) is the same rotate-domain-while-frame-fixed pattern the book flags in the learner, worn tutor-side. Feature request, not a retroactive excuse — the existing trip-wire already caught world_005 at turn 25.
